original_hypothesis.title: burn() Transfers Zero userToken When Pool Has No userToken Reserves, Potentially Reverting and Locking LP Funds
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: When a pool has zero reserveUserToken (e.g., no fee swaps have occurred yet, or all userTokens have been rebalanced out), burn() calculates amountUserToken = liquidity * 0 / totalSupply = 0 and calls IERC20(userToken).transfer(to, 0). Some ERC-20 implementations revert on zero-value transfers (e.g., USDT on certain chains, BNB). If this reverts, the entire burn transaction fails, permanently locking the LP's funds since they cannot withdraw. There is no alternative withdrawal path.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: The burn function always transfers both tokens regardless of calculated amounts. Lines ~243-244 show both transfers happen unconditionally. A zero-value transfer revert blocks the entire withdrawal.
deployment_assumptions: userToken must be a token that reverts on zero-value transfer
original_hypothesis.config_evidence: No check for zero amount before transfer at FeeAMM.sol lines 243-244; some tokens (USDT, BNB) are known to revert on zero transfers

proof_of_signal.pattern: IERC20(userToken).transfer(to, amountUserToken) called unconditionally; amountUserToken can be 0 when reserveUserToken is 0

preconditions: Pool has zero or negligible reserveUserToken (common state: pool just created, no fee swaps yet)
userToken reverts on zero-value transfer (e.g., USDT on some chains)
LP wants to withdraw via burn()

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Unconditional transfer of both tokens without zero-amount check
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: amountUserToken = liquidity * reserveUserToken / totalSupply = 0 when reserveUserToken = 0; transfer(to, 0) reverts on some tokens
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No conditional check: if (amountUserToken > 0) before transfer

analysis_questions: Which target-chain tokens revert on zero-value transfer?
Can the LP work around this by doing a tiny executeFeeSwap first to make reserveUserToken > 0?
Is this a permanent lock or can the LP recover via other means?
validation_requirements: Test burn() with a token that reverts on zero transfer when reserveUserToken = 0
Verify LP funds are permanently locked

original_hypothesis.preliminary_attack_scenario: Legitimate pool created with USDT as userToken. LPs deposit validatorToken. Before any fee swaps occur (reserveUserToken = 0), an LP tries to burn. burn() calls USDT.transfer(to, 0) which reverts. LP funds are locked. Even if someone later does a feeSwap, the dust amounts from rounding could still produce zero for small burns.
prerequisites: Token that reverts on zero transfer
Pool state where one reserve is zero

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: LP funds permanently locked in pool until reserveUserToken becomes non-zero
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All LPs in pools with zero userToken reserves and zero-revert userToken
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Partial - someone could do a minimal feeSwap to make reserveUserToken > 0, enabling burns

original_hypothesis.test_coverage_gap: No tests for burn with zero reserves or zero-revert tokens

technical_investigation_focus.capital_requirements: None - this is a DoS condition
technical_investigation_focus.execution_timing: Occurs naturally when no fee swaps have happened
technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Detectable by checking pool reserve state before burn attempt

validation_result.impact_summary: When reserveUserToken is zero (the default state after minting), burn() unconditionally calls IERC20(userToken).transfer(to, 0), which reverts on tokens that reject zero-value transfers (e.g., USDT on some chains), blocking LP withdrawals.
validation_result.detailed_analysis: The hypothesis accurately identifies a code defect in the burn() function. After mint(), only reserveValidatorToken is non-zero (mint only deposits validatorToken). reserveUserToken remains 0 until an executeFeeSwap occurs. When an LP calls burn() in this state, _calculateBurnAmounts computes amountUserToken = liquidity * 0 / totalSupply = 0. The subsequent IERC20(userToken).transfer(to, 0) call is executed unconditionally. Some ERC-20 implementations (notably USDT on certain chains, BNB) revert on zero-value transfers. This causes the entire burn transaction to revert, preventing LP withdrawal. The defect is a missing zero-amount guard before the transfer call. While a workaround exists (someone could perform an executeFeeSwap to make reserveUserToken > 0), the code is still defective because it fails to handle a legitimate and common pool state. The same issue theoretically applies to amountValidatorToken, though it's far less likely to be zero since LPs deposit validatorToken.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: The proof_of_signal is correct. The burn() function performs unconditional transfers of both tokens. _calculateBurnAmounts performs proportional math that yields 0 when the corresponding reserve is 0. mint() only ever increases reserveValidatorToken, never reserveUserToken, confirming that reserveUserToken == 0 is the natural initial state of any pool.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: Both transfers are unconditional. When reserveUserToken == 0, amountUserToken == 0, and the transfer(to, 0) call will revert on tokens that reject zero-value transfers, blocking the entire burn transaction.
When pool.reserveUserToken == 0, amountUserToken is computed as 0. This zero value is then passed to the unconditional transfer call in burn().

mint() only updates reserveValidatorToken, never reserveUserToken. This confirms that after initial pool creation, reserveUserToken remains 0 until an executeFeeSwap occurs, making the zero-transfer scenario the default state.

calling_sequence: 1. LP calls burn(userToken, validatorToken, liquidity, to)
2. Passes checks: userToken != validatorToken, liquidity != 0, liquidityBalances >= liquidity
6. IERC20(userToken).transfer(to, 0) — REVERTS if userToken rejects zero-value transfers
7. Entire burn() transaction reverts, LP cannot withdraw
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: The path is: burn() → _calculateBurnAmounts() returns amountUserToken=0 when reserveUserToken=0 → burn() calls IERC20(userToken).transfer(to, 0) unconditionally → revert on tokens that reject zero transfers. The key issue is at the transfer call in burn() which lacks a conditional check: `if (amountUserToken > 0) IERC20(userToken).transfer(to, amountUserToken);`

deployment_assumptions_checked: userToken must be a token that reverts on zero-value transfer
config_validation.deployment_evidence: No deployment scripts provided that specify which tokens will be used. The foundry.toml only configures compilation settings. The contract is described as an AMM for 'TIP-20 USD stablecoins' but no token whitelist or validation exists in the code — any ERC-20 address can be used.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: The scenario is valid and requires no attacker — it occurs naturally. Steps: (1) Pool is created via mint() with some token pair, depositing validatorToken. reserveUserToken = 0. (2) Before any executeFeeSwap occurs, an LP calls burn() to withdraw. (3) amountUserToken = 0 is computed. (4) IERC20(userToken).transfer(to, 0) is called. (5) If userToken reverts on zero transfers, the entire burn fails. The LP's funds are locked until someone performs an executeFeeSwap to make reserveUserToken > 0. This is not an attack per se but a DoS condition arising from a code defect.
validation_result.impact_assessment: MEDIUM severity. LP funds are temporarily locked when the pool's reserveUserToken is zero and the userToken rejects zero-value transfers. A workaround exists: anyone can perform a small executeFeeSwap to inject some userToken into the pool, enabling burns. However, this requires external coordination and the LP cannot unilaterally withdraw. The defect affects all pools using tokens that revert on zero transfers, during the period before any fee swaps have occurred.
recommended_mitigations: Add zero-amount guard before each transfer in burn(): `if (amountUserToken > 0) IERC20(userToken).transfer(to, amountUserToken);`
Apply the same pattern for amountValidatorToken for completeness
Use OpenZeppelin's SafeERC20 library (safeTransfer) for all token transfers, which handles non-standard return values and other edge cases
Consider adding the zero-amount check in executeFeeSwap for amountIn as well (currently no check for amountIn == 0)
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Deploy a mock ERC-20 that reverts on transfer(to, 0). 2. Deploy FeeAMM. 3. Call mint() to create a pool with the mock token as userToken and a standard token as validatorToken. 4. Call burn() with any liquidity amount. 5. Observe revert on IERC20(userToken).transfer(to, 0). 6. Verify LP cannot withdraw.
uncertainties: Which specific tokens deployed on the target chain(s) actually revert on zero-value transfers — the behavior varies by chain and token version
Whether the protocol documentation specifies a token whitelist that would exclude problematic tokens
Whether the 'TIP-20 USD stablecoins' mentioned in the NatSpec have standard ERC-20 behavior including accepting zero transfers
additional_testing_needed: Test burn() with USDT (various chain deployments) as userToken when reserveUserToken = 0
Test burn() with BNB as userToken when reserveUserToken = 0
Test the workaround: verify executeFeeSwap can unblock burn by making reserveUserToken > 0
Fuzz test: burn with various liquidity amounts when reserveUserToken is very small (rounding to 0 scenario)
